speaker: interviewer
text: It is December the 29th, 1996. The survivor is Kathy Levy. The interviewer is Hermann Hirschberger. We are located in London, England, and the language is English. My name is Hermann Hirschberger. I am interviewing Kathy Levy. The location is London, England, and the language is English. Could you please give your full name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Kathy Levy, K-A-T-H-Y, Levy, L-E-V-Y. My maiden name is Hermann, H-E-R-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have a middle name-- Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24th of March, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: The place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father and your mother, what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Alexander Hermann. My mother was Elizabeth Honti, H-O-N-T-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were both Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were both born in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in which city were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was actually born in Budapest. My father was born in a smaller town called Paks, P-A-K-S, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a very good position in a big industrial farm business. And that's where he worked until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did commercial work, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes-- office work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in some sort of senior position?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite a good position, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you say you were in reasonably good circumstances in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we weren't well off, but I mean, we weren't short of anything, really-- reasonably good circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part of Budapest did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the center of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah-ha. Well, what sort of home did you have there--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had--

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: --flats, because in Budapest, most people live in flats. Not many-- not many people live in houses. It's only on the other side, on Buda's side. On the Pest side, there are people-- live in flats. And we had a nice three-apartment flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three bedroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were not so many bedrooms there-- just three apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah-ha. I see-- three rooms--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three rooms, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in-- in the apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this modern by the standards of that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was reasonably nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live there throughout your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less-- in two different flats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what sort of memories you have-- your earliest childhood memories-- as far back as you can go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have many memories, because I'm very forgetful by now. But whatever I have, they are good memories, because I was-- I come from a happy family, and I had a happy childhood. I-- I loved my schools, my friends. And surprisingly enough, up till the age of 18, I never felt the anti-Semitism, because I only mixed with Jewish people. I went to Jewish primary school. I went to Jewish secondary school. All my friends were Jewish. And I didn't feel the anti-Semitism. It's only when I was grown up. I mean, when I left school, that's when I started feeling it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any specific memories of your childhood-- as early as you can go-- about your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had been brought up-- I had a cousin. My late mother had a sister, and she had a daughter who was three months younger than I was. And we were brought up like twins. We did everything together with my cousin until the deportation. Because after that, then our paths separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have no brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: No brothers--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and sisters, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No siblings.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what sort of activities did your parents engage in? Were they secular activities, or was it a religious home? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a particularly religious home. We were-- we were a good, observant Jewish family. I don't know whether we traveled on the Shabbat. I can't really remember anymore, because it was a number of years ago. [LAUGHS] And-- but we kept a Jewish home. And we kept all the Jewish holidays. And we did go to the synagogue every Shabbat. First I went, because I was going to the Jewish school, and I had to go to the school synagogue. And later on, I went because my friends were all going. So it was a social occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other social life in Budapest in the early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes-- the normal social life for-- for young girls-- lots of friends, boyfriends, going out, doing things together, going on holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a fairly sophisticated environment in Budapest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, I wouldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a world city.

speaker: interviewee
text: --call it very-- not-- not in today's standard. It wasn't really as sophisticated as what we call today sophisticated. But it was a-- I was a happy, happy youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have any interest in the arts or sports? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very keen on football, and my mother was keen on theater. They-- they went to the theater quite often. And otherwise, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents spoke Hungarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only.

speaker: interviewer
text: --only.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Could they speak other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father spoke German-- not perfect-- but he spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you learn--

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned German, because already, when I was a little girl, we had a-- with my cousin together-- we had a nanny-- kind-- somebody who was looking after us, taking us for walks, and so on. And she was a German lady-- so that we should learn German. And in school, at a very early age, already I think at the age of 12, we started learning English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of information media did you live with-- newspapers, radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little when I was a youngster-- I wasn't really interested in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents, did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My parents-- the radio, yes, radio and newspapers, yes. But not me. I was too young for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a grandmother. I didn't know my father's parents-- I didn't know at all. My mother's side, my grandfather, I can't really remember him. I was 5-years-old when he passed away. But my grandmother, she lived till the age of 96. And we brought her to Scotland, and she died here in Scotland in 1971, I think. Even my son Robert knew my late grandmother. So he knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was his great-grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she live at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, she lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived with us always, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you on good terms with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very. I was very, very fond of her, and we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: --very great.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of her in those days? Was she a good cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. She was a great cook. And what we always talk about that nowadays, for instance, elderly people, they wear-- as myself-- I am wearing red now. My grandmother never wore anything else but black or grey after her husband passed away. She was then in her early 60s, or maybe not even 60, in her 50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from that day on, she never wore any other color but black or grey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that was a conscious thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. In those days, I think that was the fashion. When somebody loses a partner, then you are mourning all your life. But she was a wonderful old lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did people not remarry sometimes in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I suppose they did. She never did. She never did. She never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she-- did she have a lot of Jewish consciousness, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, Yes. And she had-- she came from a family of 12. They were 12 brothers and sisters. And I remember some of them-- not all of them. They lived in the country in a little village, and we spent many holidays there. My grandmother took me and my cousin many times to the little village where her sisters lived. Two sisters lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far from Budapest was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it was about 50 kilometer, but for us, that was a great distance in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went by train, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- did your mother have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes. And my mother had a sister whose daughter was my cousin, who we were brought up together. That was my cousin. And my mother had two brothers. One lived in Hungary, and the other left Hungary at a very early age. He was working in a big circus. He was an acrobat, actually, to begin with. And then in the-- I think it was in the late '30s, he married a girl from Vienna. And then just before the war started, they went and settled in Scotland. And that's how we went to Scotland eventually, because that's-- my mother's brother lived in Scotland, and he helped us to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say he was an acrobat. Did other members of the family engage--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in athletic--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: --pursuits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Nobody. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was coincidence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In those earlier years between-- as far as you can remember-- the late '20s and early '30s-- were you aware of anything at all political?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing, nothing. I was a child living a happy childhood-- had my friends, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall whether your parents in any way took an interest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And subsequently, in the '30s, did you become aware-- although you weren't politically knowledgeable-- did you become aware of things happening in other parts of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well,   we had  to. We had to, unfortunately. It had to come. And we had to be aware of it, especially in the late '30s when I was about 16 and 17. Then we were aware of it, what was going on. But we still couldn't believe it, that anything will happen to us because of our Jewishness. We never, never believed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, in your visits to the synagogue or community, were there any discussions about these things? Do you recall anything specific?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I can't recall it. Possibly, I have forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the year 1939 came, do you remember when the war started, the war in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely, yes, yes. Although, Hungary was not involved at all in the war. But oh, yes, we did. We do remember, because we have read it in the papers, and it was on the radio. We had no television in those days yet. But we knew about it. We knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people in Hungary of your age group, or perhaps your parents, know about happenings in Germany to Jews at that stage, late '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not much, not much, not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was not much known about Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We heard about the Kristallnacht. But about deportations and things like that, we didn't know much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you recall-- after the war started in Europe, your life continued as normally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolute normally until 1943. Then I finished school. I graduated from school. And I would have liked to study, but that was impossible, because Jewish people couldn't go and study-- couldn't go for higher education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that stage, in 1943, anti-Semitism was overt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Already yes. It was very ripe. And in those times, already there were the-- how should I call it-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish laws that Jews couldn't take certain jobs. Jews couldn't do-- go to certain places. So these were quite ripe in Hungary by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these laws, do you think, motivated by what happened in Germany or did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I'm sure. I'm sure. I'm sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents, at that stage, which is now a bit later, show any fear or anxiety?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. But we had nobody in a foreign country. We had no relations, because we had very little contact with that one brother of my mother's who was abroad. And we never even thought about it to leave Hungary. Why should we?

speaker: interviewer
text: So 1943 was the start of your becoming seriously aware--

speaker: interviewee
text: Aware, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of things not being right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even at that stage, did you dismiss it or think about it seriously?

speaker: interviewee
text: I dismissed it, because I was 18, a young girl, and I was only interested in my enjoyment, and my entertainment, and my friends. And I didn't think about it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the time 1943 came, and it became a more serious prospect, did your parents indicate anything of anxiety?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I don't think so. Because if they would have done it, then I-- maybe I would remember more. But my father, already in 19-- for the second half of 1943, my father already has been called up for going to a work camp. And he was away from home for many, many months. And he went. Actually, he was deported from the work camp street. He was taken to Germany to the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the work camps-- were they initially for Jews only?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before the Germans invaded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was at home, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with your father when he was at the work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much-- only very occasionally. If somebody-- maybe for some reason-- somebody became ill, and then they had-- they released him. And then they brought messages. Very occasionally we had a letter, but not very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him during those months?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which month he went to the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been around the same time when I went-- or a little bit earlier. Because still, in 1944, in the summer of 1944, he was still in the work camp. So it was in the autumn of 1944. That's when they started, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, by then '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the Germans invaded--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Hungary on March the 19th in '44. And first, only the people from the country, from the provinces, were taken to the camps. And the people in Budapest were still spared until about September. That's when they started taking the people from Budapest to the camps. And that's when my father was taken from the work camp, and he went to Mauthausen and Buchenwald. And he was liberated in Buchenwald eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going back to autumn, 1944, that was when your father was moved to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long previously had he been in the labor--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: --camp? About a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But during that year, he lived reasonably?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Because they were working, and they were always on different locations where they have been working. And they were still getting food and so on. So it was all right yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But no leave to come home.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that year when he was in the labor camp, did your mother or you feel anxiety or fear-- strong fear for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly. I have no recollection of it, but possibly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're now in autumn, 1944. You have heard that your father has been sent--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was first taken in August 1944. The young people were taken to working-- work camps as well. So I was taken to the provinces, not very far from Budapest, with a friend of mine who lived in the same block of house where we lived. And by then, we had to leave our own house, and we had to move into the Jewish houses. Because there were some districts and some houses designated to be Jewish houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this what you might call a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. Yes, let's call it a ghetto. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was August '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And our own home was not designated for that. But I had an uncle, my mother's brother, and their flat, their building, was designated-- and that area-- as a Jewish house. So we had to move in with them. And from there-- there I made friends with a girl who was the same age as me, and we were taken to the work camp. And we were there for-- from about September, October. We came back about the middle of November. From there, we escaped. And somebody helped us back to Budapest with my friend. And two weeks later, I was-- we were again collected, and I was taken to Bergen-Belsen on the 4th of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you return to your home when you escaped from the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where else could I go? Where else should I go? I had nobody else, only my mother and my grandmother. That was my only family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that your father had been deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. By then, we did know. We did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just to come back to your father, because he was sent away in autumn '44. He was first sent to Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you hear any more-- anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything about it. We had no idea. When I went to Bergen-Belsen, it was the first time in my life that I heard about a place like Bergen-Belsen. I never knew that that existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew he'd been sent to an-- as far as you were concerned-- another labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know where we are-- only when we arrived there. Then we were told that this is a concentration camp and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: His history at Buchenwald, was he stayed there for some-- yeah, for some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He stayed there for many, many months. I don't know how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you when were they taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And he was liberated--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, coming back to yourself, when you were sent to the labor camp in August 1944, what sort of thoughts did you have about that? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were-- we were afraid. We didn't know what's going to happen to us there. We were very much afraid. We were living in-- in-- terrible-- under terrible circumstances in-- in-- in barns, on-- on straw. We were sleeping on the straw. And here were-- we were sleeping here, and there with the cows beside us. And it was terrible circumstances. It wasn't a very pleasant time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was food, yes, yes. There was food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you mistreated there, physically?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Physically, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Hungarian guards, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So does that mean you lived behind barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, because we went out and worked on the fields. We had to do work on the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back in November, you broke out from the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. It was a nun who brought us back with my friend, because her family was very friendly with that nun. And she came and brought some false papers, and that's how she brought us back to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother know you were coming back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were hoping. They didn't know exactly when we will arrive. But they knew that we-- she's trying to bring us back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those two weeks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back home? Ah, I can't recall anymore those two weeks, because the end of it wasn't very pleasant when we were taken away. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me of it, about the day you were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The 4th of December, that was my father's birthday, and my little grandson's birthday is the 4th of December. So I can never forget that date. And we were taken to the station. And we were put into open wagons, about 60 of us into a wagon. And we traveled for two weeks in the open wagons-- 14 days. Sometimes for days we stopped just somewhere, anywhere. We have no idea. We don't know what route we have been going on. We haven't seen any of the stations, because you couldn't see out from the-- you see,  the wall of the wagons was that high. And we couldn't see out. And for 14 days we were traveling in the wagon. Sanitary-- that-- that was absolutely-- you can't even talk about it. We were young people like myself. There were families, mothers with children, young children. There were elderly people-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --all mixed up, everything mixed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The time in the-- in the truck of the train, were you guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only we couldn't get down. I don't know what guards-- some guards were traveling with us on the train, but we didn't even see them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: During, though, that journey, was there a certain amount of food available, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose so, because we survived. But we did take some food from home, and they haven't taken that food away from us. So at least the food we had-- what we have taken from home. And occasionally, occasionally, we had some food from-- on-- on the wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people bring a certain--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not people.

speaker: interviewer
text: --amount of luggage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we all had a little luggage. But eventually, it was everything lost in the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your arrival.

speaker: interviewee
text: My arrival in Belsen? It was the 14th-- the-- no, no-- 10 days we were traveling. I arrived on the 14th of December. And-- well, we got out of the wagons, and we saw the camp. And it was a brand new experience.
